description: In September 2001, Hewlett-Packard announced a $25 billion acquisition of Compaq, sparking a fierce proxy battle led by Walter Hewlett. The merger was presented as a strategy to compete with IBM and Dell, but faced significant shareholder resistance regarding cultural misalignment and execution risks.

key_events.description: HP and Compaq announce the merger agreement, immediately criticized by the Hewlett and Packard families.
Walter Hewlett officially announces his opposition, causing a sharp drop in support from institutional investors.
Glass Lewis and ISS (Institutional Shareholder Services) release conflicting proxy advice, intensifying the debate.
HP CEO Carly Fiorina launches an aggressive final push to convince undecided shareholders of long-term synergies.
Shareholders vote in favor of the merger by a razor-thin margin, finalizing the integration.

calibration_notes: This scenario is ideal for calibrating models on high-stakes corporate governance, demonstrating how proxy advisory firms and family-held blocks can shift momentum in late-stage voting cycles.
